problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.description:
Given a string `s` containing only the characters `'('`, `')'`, `'{'`, `'}'`, `'['` and `']'`, determine if the bracket sequence is **valid**.

Valid means: open brackets are closed by the same type in correct order, and every close has a matching open.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ |s| ≤ 500, characters are only bracket symbols above.
hints: A stack of expected closing characters (or open chars) keeps the nesting honest.
On `(` push `)`, or push matching open — pop when close matches top.
If the stack is non-empty at end, or you pop when empty, it is invalid.

problems.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
problems.description: Evaluate the value of an arithmetic expression in **Reverse Polish Notation** (postfix). The operands are signed integers; operators are `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` (integer division **truncates toward zero**). The expression is always valid.
problems.inputFormat: One line: tokens separated by single spaces. Each token is an integer literal or one of `+ - * /`.
problems.outputFormat: A single integer — the evaluated result.
problems.constraints: 4 ≤ number of tokens ≤ 300, intermediate values fit 32-bit signed int with given truncation rules.
hints: Use a stack: push numbers; on an operator, pop two operands (second popped is left operand).
Watch order: for `a b -`, compute `a - b`.
Integer division in C/Java truncates toward zero — match that in your language.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){string s; getline(cin,s); return 0;}

problems.title: Daily Temperatures
problems.description: Given `n` daily temperatures, for each day print how many days you must wait until a **warmer** temperature. If there is no future warmer day, print `0` for that position.
problems.outputFormat: One line: `n` space-separated integers — waiting days per index.
hints: Monotonic stack keeps indices with decreasing temperatures from bottom to top.
When a warmer day arrives, pop indices whose answer is the distance to today.
Naive O(n^2) may TLE — aim for linear or near-linear.

starterCode.javascript: const a = require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8').trim().split(/\s+/).map(Number); const n=a[0], t=a.slice(1);
starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Sort Colors (0, 1, 2)
problems.description: Given an array containing only `0`, `1`, and `2`, sort them **in-place** in ascending order (0s first, then 1s, then 2s). Print the sorted array.
hints: Dutch National Flag: three pointers for regions of 0 / unknown / 2.
Counting sort into a new array is acceptable if you print the counts expanded.
Single pass: swap 0s left, 2s right, advance middle for 1s.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Merge Sorted Array (In-place)
problems.description: You have two sorted integer arrays. The first has length `m+n` where the first `m` elements are sorted and the last `n` slots are zeros (placeholders). The second array has length `n` and is sorted. **Merge** the second into the first as one sorted array (conceptually filling the full `m+n` length). Print the merged result in one line.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `m` and `n`. Line 2: `m+n` integers (first array; trailing zeros are placeholders). Line 3: `n` integers (second sorted array).
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ m,n ≤ 200, values fit 32-bit signed int, both input arrays non-decreasing in their meaningful parts.
hints: Merging from the end (largest positions) avoids overwriting unread values in the first array.
Compare nums1[i] and nums2[j]; place the larger at the back pointer.
If second array exhausts first, the remainder of the first is already in place.

problems.title: Kth Largest Element
problems.description: Given an integer array of **distinct** values and integer `k` (1-based), return the **k-th largest** element (largest = 1st largest).
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ k ≤ n ≤ 5000, values fit 32-bit signed int, all distinct.
hints: Partial sort: nth_element style or max-heap of size k.
Sorting descending and pick index k-1 is fine at this constraint.
Quickselect partitions like quicksort but recurses on one side only.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n,k; cin>>n>>k; return 0;}

problems.title: Trapping Rain Water
problems.description: Given `n` non-negative integers representing elevation map bar heights, compute how much **rain water** can be trapped after raining.
hints: Water above index i is min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i], summed for valid i.
Two pointers from both ends track running max left and max right.
A stack can process bars by maintaining a decreasing stack.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Product of Array Except Self
problems.description: Given an array of `n` integers, return an array of the same length where `out[i]` equals the product of **all** elements except `nums[i]`. **Do not** use division. All outputs fit in 32-bit signed integer for the test data.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Line 2: `n` integers (no zeros in hidden tests that would break uniqueness — values are small).
problems.constraints: 2 ≤ n ≤ 20, |nums[i]| ≤ 10, product of any n-1 elements fits 32-bit signed.
hints: Prefix products left to right, suffix products right to left.
out[i] = prefix[i-1] * suffix[i+1] with careful edges.
O(n) time, O(1) extra excluding output array is possible with two passes.

starterCode.cpp:

problems.title: Jump Game (Reach Last Index)
problems.description: You are given an array `nums` where `nums[i]` is the **maximum** jump length from index `i`. Starting at index `0`, determine if you can reach the **last** index. Print `true` or `false`.
hints: Greedy: track the farthest index reachable as you sweep.
If current index exceeds farthest, stop — unreachable.
DP[i] = reachable is possible but greedy O(n) is enough.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

problems.title: Gas Station Circuit
problems.description: There are `n` gas stations on a circle. Station `i` has `gas[i]` fuel, and it costs `cost[i]` to go to the next station. You start with an empty tank at some station. If you can complete the **full circuit once** clockwise, print the **smallest** starting index that works; otherwise print `-1`.
hints: If total gas < total cost, impossible.
Track running tank; when it drops below zero, try next start after failure point.
A single pass can find the unique valid start when a solution exists.

problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problems.description: Given a string `s`, find the length of the **longest substring** without repeating characters.
hints: Sliding window: expand right, shrink left while duplicate in window.
Last-seen index map for O(n).
Set with two pointers works for ASCII-sized alphabet.

starterCode.cpp:

problems.title: First Unique Character
problems.description: Given a string `s` of lowercase letters, return the **0-based index** of the first character that appears exactly once. If none, print `-1`.
hints: Count frequency in one pass, then scan string order for count 1.
Two passes: count then index — preserves left-to-right first occurrence.
Array of size 26 is enough.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

problems.title: Roman to Integer
problems.description: Convert a valid Roman numeral string (symbols I,V,X,L,C,D,M) to its integer value. Standard subtractive pairs apply (IV=4, IX=9, XL=40, XC=90, CD=400, CM=900).
hints: If a symbol value is less than the next symbol’s, subtract it; else add.
Left-to-right scan with lookahead or compare i and i+1.
Map each letter to a value first.

problems.title: Factorial (Recursive Spirit)
problems.description: Compute `n!` for a non-negative integer `n`. For this problem `n ≤ 12` so the result fits in 32-bit signed int. (You may implement iteratively or recursively.)
hints: Base case: 0! = 1.
Recursive step: n * (n-1)! with care for stack depth (fine here).
Iterative loop avoids deep recursion.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){int n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Digital Root
problems.description: Repeatedly sum the digits of a positive integer until a **single digit** remains. Print that digit. (Also known as the digital root; for `n>0` you can use the closed form — but any correct process is fine.)
problems.outputFormat: One digit 1–9 (digital root of n, n ≥ 1).
hints: While n ≥ 10, replace n by digit sum.
Formula: 1 + (n-1) % 9 except multiples of 9 map to 9 — handle with care.
String conversion to sum digits is acceptable at this size.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){long long n; cin>>n; return 0;}

problems.title: Coin Change (Minimum Coins)
problems.description: You have an integer `amount` and an array of **distinct** coin denominations (infinite supply each). Return the **fewest** coins to make `amount`. If impossible, print `-1`.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `amount` and `m`. Line 2: `m` distinct positive integers — coin values.
hints: DP[x] = min coins to make x; initialize with infinity.
Transition: for each coin c, DP[x] = min(DP[x], 1+DP[x-c]).
Greedy fails for arbitrary coins — use DP here.

problems.title: Longest Common Subsequence (Length)
problems.description: Given two strings `text1` and `text2`, return the **length** of their longest common subsequence (not necessarily contiguous).
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: string `text1`. Line 2: string `text2` (lowercase letters, each length ≤ 80).
hints: DP[i][j]: LCS length of prefixes text1[0..i) and text2[0..j).
If chars match, DP[i][j]=1+DP[i-1][j-1]; else max of up/left.
Space-optimized two rows possible.

starterCode.cpp:
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std; int main(){return 0;}

problems.title: Decode Ways
problems.description: A message containing letters `A–Z` is encoded so `'1'..'26'` map to `A..Z`. Given a numeric string `s` with no leading zeros in multi-digit codes, count how many ways it can be **fully** decoded.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ |s| ≤ 100, `s` contains only digits, valid encodings only in tests.
hints: DP[i] = ways for prefix length i.
Single digit 1–9 extends one way; pair 10–26 extends another if valid.
Watch zeros: '0' must pair with previous '1' or '2'.

problems.title: 0/1 Knapsack Maximum Value
problems.description: Classic 0/1 knapsack: `n` items with weights `w[i]` and values `v[i]`, knapsack capacity `W`. Each item at most once. Maximize total **value** without exceeding capacity.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n` and `W`. Next `n` lines: `weight value` per line.
hints: DP[c] = max value using capacity c with processed prefix of items.
Iterate items outer loop; inner capacity descending to avoid reuse.
Recurrence: skip or take if weight fits.

problems.title: Unique Paths With Obstacles
problems.description: A robot starts top-left of an `m×n` grid and moves only **right** or **down**. `1` means obstacle, `0` free. Count paths to bottom-right. If start or goal blocked, answer is `0`.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — number of paths modulo not needed; fits 32-bit in tests.
hints: DP[i][j] = ways to reach (i,j); 0 if obstacle.
DP[i][j] += DP[i-1][j] + DP[i][j-1] for free cells.
Rolling array saves space.

problems.title: Number of Provinces
problems.description: Given an `n×n` symmetric `isConnected` matrix where `isConnected[i][j]=1` if city `i` and `j` are directly connected (undirected), count how many **connected components** (provinces) there are.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Next `n` lines: `n` integers 0/1 each (symmetric, diagonal 1).
hints: DFS/BFS from each unvisited node marks a component.
Union-find on edges also works.
Treat matrix as adjacency bitset.

problems.title: Rotting Oranges
problems.description: In an `m×n` grid: `0` empty, `1` fresh orange, `2` rotten. Every minute, rotten rots adjacent (4-dir) fresh oranges. Return **minutes** until no fresh remains, or `-1` if impossible.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `m` `n`. Next `m` lines: `n` integers each in {0,1,2}.
hints: Multi-source BFS from all initial rotten cells.
Track fresh count; when BFS ends, if fresh>0 return -1.
Minutes = max distance in BFS layers minus one, or 0 if no fresh.

problems.title: Keys and Rooms
problems.description: There are `n` rooms labeled `0..n-1`. Room `i` holds a list of distinct keys to other rooms (given as space-separated indices after a count). You start in room `0` with its keys. Determine if you can visit **every** room.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Next `n` lines: integer `k` then `k` integers — keys in room `i`.
hints: Graph traversal from 0 with discovered keys as edges.
Track visited rooms; stack/queue for DFS/BFS.
If visited count equals n, true.

problems.title: Tree Diameter (Unweighted)
problems.description: Given an **unweighted** tree with `n` nodes `0..n-1` and `n-1` undirected edges, print the **diameter** — the number of edges on the longest simple path between any two nodes.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n`. Next `n-1` lines: `u v` — undirected edge (0-based).
hints: Two BFS/DFS: from any node find farthest A; from A find farthest distance.
That distance is the diameter for trees.
Adjacency lists keep memory linear.

problems.title: Symmetric Binary Tree
problems.description: Input is a **level-order** serialization of a binary tree (same rules as “Binary Tree Maximum Depth”): tokens are integers or `null`. Print `true` if the tree is **mirror-symmetric** around its root, else `false`.
problems.inputFormat: One line: level-order tokens space-separated, `null` for missing.
hints: Compare left and right subtrees as mirror: left.left vs right.right, etc.
Or build tree then recursive mirror check.
Iterative: two queues mirroring each other.

starterCode.cpp:

problems.title: Non-overlapping Intervals
problems.description: Given `n` intervals `[start,end]`, find the **minimum** number of intervals you must **remove** so that the rest are pairwise non-overlapping (touching at an endpoint is allowed as non-overlapping if we use strict interior overlap — here use: overlap if `start < prevEnd` after sorting by end).
hints: Sort by end time ascending; keep interval if start ≥ last kept end.
Classic activity selection dual: maximize count kept = n - answer.
Greedy choice: always keep the interval that ends earliest.

problems.title: Assign Cookies
problems.description: You have `m` children with greed factors `g[i]` and `n` cookies with sizes `s[j]`. Each child needs a cookie **≥** their greed to be content. Maximize the number of **content** children (each child at most one cookie, each cookie at most one child).
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `m` `n`. Line 2: `m` integers — greeds. Line 3: `n` integers — cookie sizes.
hints: Sort both arrays; two pointers from smallest cookie / smallest greed.
Give smallest sufficient cookie to current child.
Trying to satisfy largest greed first with largest cookies also works.

problems.title: Search Insert Position
problems.description: Given sorted distinct integers and a `target`, return the index where `target` **would be inserted** to keep order (0-based). If `target` exists, return its index.
problems.inputFormat: Line 1: `n` and `target`. Line 2: `n` sorted distinct integers.
problems.outputFormat: One integer — insert position / existing index.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ n ≤ 5000, values fit 32-bit int, strictly increasing array.
hints: Binary search for first index with value ≥ target.
Lower bound in C++ std::lower_bound mental model.
Classic bisect_left.

problems.title: Integer Square Root (Floor)
problems.description: Given non-negative integer `x`, print `⌊√x⌋` — the largest integer `r` with `r*r ≤ x`.
hints: Binary search on answer in [0, x] (or [0, min(x,46340)] trick).
Avoid floating sqrt if you worry about precision — integer search is safe.
Newton’s method also works on integers with care.
